pimpQuestions: Q: What are the two ways in which a carpal tunnel release can be performed? A: Open or endoscopic
Q: What is the operative treatment of carpal tunnel syndrome? A: Release of the transcarpal ligament (open, mini open, arthroscopic)
Q: Is there any difference in outcomes following open vs endoscopic carpal tunnel release? A: Patients with endoscopic surgery return to work earlier; no difference in long-term patient outcomes.
Q: What is the most common reason for failed endoscopic carpal tunnel surgery? A: Incomplete release of the transverse carpal ligament distally.

Q: How do outcomes of endoscopic and open carpal tunnel release compare? A: Endoscopic may have less early scar tenderness, improved short term function, and better patient satisfaction scores; long term results are equivalent.
Q: What are the contents of the carpal tunnel? A: FDS tendons x4, FDP tendons x4, FPL, Median nerve.
Q: Which structure may be injured if the transverse carpal ligament is incised too radially? A: Recurrent motor branch of the median nerve.
Q: What is the most sensitive test for diagnosing carpal tunnel syndrome? A: Durkan's compression test.
Q: What is the most radial structure within the carpal tunnel? A: FPL.
Q: In the carpal tunnel, the median nerve runs in between what? A: FDS and FCR (FCR is outside of carpal tunnel).
otherUsefulFacts: No additional benefit gained from neurolysis or tenosynovectomy in carpal tunnel syndrome.
Steroids yield transient relief in 80% of patients after 6 weeks, but only 20% are symptom free at 1 year.
Acute carpal tunnel may occur with a distal radius fracture; this is a surgical emergency.

selected.rationale: Specifically designed for carpal tunnel release, targeting the transverse carpal ligament and median nerve at the wrist.

approachSelection.notes: No other approaches in the catalog are relevant to carpal tunnel surgery.

questions.q: What superficial nerves are encountered during the carpal tunnel approach?
Which ligament is released in the carpal tunnel approach?
Name three structures at risk during the carpal tunnel approach.
questions.answer: Median nerve, recurrent motor branch, palmar cutaneous branch

questions.q: Is the carpal tunnel approach performed through an internervous plane?
questions.answer: No, it is not an internervous plane

questions.q: What motor branch of the median nerve is at risk during the carpal tunnel release?
questions.answer: Recurrent motor branch of the median nerve

questions.q: What cutaneous branch of the median nerve is at risk in a carpal tunnel approach?
The carpal tunnel approach is classified as which type of surgical approach based on location?
For which clinical procedure is the carpal tunnel approach primarily used?
